description_long: This is a sleek, futuristic airplane with a sharply contoured, angular fuselage and a wide, swept-back wing configuration. The body is predominantly dark, with a glossy finish that accentuates its aerodynamic form. Large, rectangular engine intakes are mounted on either side of the fuselage, suggesting powerful propulsion, while the rear features two prominent jet exhausts. The tail section is equipped with twin vertical stabilizers and a central horizontal stabilizer, giving it a distinctive H-tail appearance. The cockpit is smoothly integrated at the front, with a dark, tinted canopy that blends seamlessly into the nose. The overall design is reminiscent of advanced military or sci-fi aircraft, optimized for speed and maneuverability.
description: A dark, glossy, futuristic airplane with swept-back wings, large side intakes, twin rear jet exhausts, and an H-tail configuration. The cockpit is smoothly integrated at the front with a tinted canopy.
description_view_0: Front view showing the pointed nose, wide swept-back wings, large side intakes, and the smoothly integrated cockpit canopy.
description_view_1: Side-angled view highlighting the angular fuselage, wing sweep, and the raised twin vertical stabilizers at the rear.
description_view_2: Rear view displaying the twin jet exhausts, H-tail configuration, and the full span of the swept-back wings.
description_view_3: Opposite side-angled view emphasizing the engine intake, wing structure, and the tail's vertical stabilizers.
attribute_wingspan_configuration: Low-wing, swept-back wings with moderate anhedral angle and no visible winglets.
attribute_engine_type: Twin jet engines integrated at the rear fuselage.
attribute_tail_design: H-tail configuration with twin vertical stabilizers and a central horizontal stabilizer.
attribute_landing_gear_type: Retractable landing gear, tricycle configuration with main and nose gear units visible.
attribute_cockpit_placement: Forward-mounted, enclosed cockpit with a single, smoothly contoured tinted canopy.
